summary: German-American abstract artist, Bauhaus instructor, and color theorist. Most commonly identified by the Homage to the Square series and the textbook Interaction of Color. Taught at Black Mountain College and Yale. Wife: textile artist Anni Albers.

works.name: Homage to the Square (1950–1976)
works.description: Series of over 1000 paintings exploring color relationships through three or four nested squares. Painted with oil on Masonite using a palette knife. Subtitles include Ascending, Joy, Glow, Skyward, Somber Promise, Earthbound. Earned the Met's first solo exhibition for a living artist in 1971. Wikipedia

comprehensive_summary:
Josef Albers was a German-American abstract artist and influential art educator associated with Op Art and geometric abstraction. He is most commonly identified as the creator of the Homage to the Square series, a monumental project spanning over 25 years (from 1949 to 1976) in which he explored color relationships through paintings of three or four nested squares on Masonite, applied with a palette knife. The series aims at the 'revelation and evocation of vision' as stated in his 8-line essay 'The Origin of Art.' Individual works bear subtitles such as Ascending (held by the Whitney), Joy, Glow, Skyward, Somber Promise, Earthbound, Precinct, Soft Spoken, and With Rays (the latter three held by the Met). Some entries were made as screenprints with tutors Sewell Sillman and Norman Ives. The series influenced the characteristic style of his student Richard Anuszkiewicz. It was partly inspired by adobe houses Albers saw in the Southwest (the Variant series) and trips to Mexico to study pre-Columbian art. The series earned him the Metropolitan Museum's first solo exhibition for a living artist in 1971.

Albers taught the introductory Vorkurs course at the Bauhaus, where Tom Wolfe memorably described his paper exercise — praising a simple tent over Gothic castles for using 'the soul of paper.' At the Bauhaus, Marcel Breuer varied on Albers's colorful 'nesting' table design by using tubular steel instead of wood. Albers met his wife, textile artist Anni Albers (née Fleischmann), at the Bauhaus glass workshop. After the Nazis closed the Bauhaus in 1933, Philip Johnson arranged for both Josef and Anni to teach at Black Mountain College near Asheville, North Carolina. There, Albers taught a Werklehre class emphasizing 'fingertip touch for materials'; Ruth Asawa studied wire work under him there. Anni forbade Josef from selling any painting using the color red, as it represented love and passion.

Albers later became chair of the Department of Design at the Yale School of Fine Arts, where he wrote his hugely influential textbook Interaction of Color (1963). The book distinguishes between 'factual fact' and 'actual fact,' contains prints demonstrating the Bezold Effect, and originally used material from wallpaper rolls and magazine pages. Its 50th anniversary edition included a companion app. At Yale, Albers also taught Eva Hesse.

Other notable works include the Structural Constellations (white-on-black wireframe engravings), the portfolio Formulation: Articulation (containing silkscreens of his Graphic Tectonics zinc plate artworks), and the mural Manhattan, commissioned by Walter Gropius for the Pan Am Building lobby. A 2017 show at the Yale Peabody Museum connected his Mesoamerican art collection to his Variant/Adobe series. A font called 'Achitype Albers' was designed based on his geometric experiments. He lived in Orange, Connecticut, and was known for taking visitors to a local restaurant called the Plank House.
